data.title: Inside Nebius's Secret GPU Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage on Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS), the stock has pulled back roughly 16%, offering investors a chance to accumulate shares near the recent $21 private placement price secured by institutional backers like Nvidia (NVDA), Accel, and Orbis. I’ve continued digging deeper into the company to better understand its strategic edge and evolving position in the AI infrastructure race. Today, I’m sharing fresh insights and findings from that ongoing research, uncovering the structural advantages that could make Nebius one of the most overlooked GPU infrastructure plays in the market.

How Nebius Quietly Built a Cost Advantage Over CoreWeave

Nebius’s business model is defined by vertical integration, geopolitical arbitrage, and hyperscaler-grade efficiency without hyperscaler-grade expenses. By designing, owning, and operating its whole AI infrastructure stack, including its data centers, custom server design, liquid-cooling systems, and GPU supply chain, Nebius is not reliant on third-party providers or artificially inflated GPU rental marketplaces like the majority of its competitors.

Not only does it reduce operational costs (e.g., sub-$0.025 per GPU-hour in Serbia), but it also raises uptime, customization, and sovereignty to its users. Its deliberate management of power, thermal design, and rack density allows it to keep GPU clusters at near-peak utilization rates, and that matters to maintain margins on inference and training workloads.

Nebius provides the lowest possible price in the GPU cloud market, facilitated by its capital position. Nebius minimizes gross margins through their ODM approach down to around 2%, which greatly minimizes initial CapEx on hardware and operational costs, for instance, for electricity. This cost-effectiveness positions Nebius in a position quite different from that of other providers that are not hyperscalers, as they utilize optimization that one only finds in hyperscale cloud providers.

A further comparative examination by PoolCompute reveals that Nebius AI has a very competitive pricing plan that may retain up to 50% of the money that users pay as opposed to leading cloud providers. For example, Nebius has Nvidia H100 GPUs available for $4.85 per hour pay-as-you-go, with deep discounts for longer periods of commitment, down to $3.15 per hour for a 12-month reservation. CoreWeave, on the other hand, has a very flexible and economical costing plan with a wide variety of Nvidia GPUs, with up to $4.76 per hour for the H100 HGX.

These results indicate that Nebius’s cost-per-FLOP will be around 40–60% less than its peers, such as CoreWeave (CRWV), making Nebius a low-cost option for GPU cloud services when customers commit for extended periods, such as 12 months. In other words, Nebius can do the same AI work as others like CoreWeave but for about half the cost. This means they’re much more efficient and can offer cheaper, faster services.

Thus, when public pricing for H100s sits around $2.00 per hour, a 2.5-cent operating cost shows just how wide the margin potential is, positioning Nebius to scale profitably once CapEx is absorbed.

The model has its imperfections, though. The biggest threat is capital intensity and long payback horizons. Proprietary data centers, GPU sourcing from gray markets, and custom firmware stacks all have high capital expenditures up front; Nebius spent nearly $2 billion in three years in CapEx alone. It delivers performance upside but raises the threat if demand weakens, sourcing streams dry up, or geopolitical tides change direction. It limits enterprise growth in the West from countries in or subject to sanctions, and many Nebius customers fall into that category. It lowers the addressable market and may curb growth in the long term.

Despite all the risks involved, the moat that emerges will be real. If Nebius continues to lock up GPUs at sub-market prices, offers sovereign AI services outside U.S. jurisdiction, and adds to its AI developer platform with offerings like Toloka and TripleTen, it will build meaningful switching costs. With data residency, sovereignty, and cost being just as relevant in a bifurcating world of AI as raw muscle, Nebius may not need to match the scale of AWS or Google (GOOG) but simply needs to own the alternative lane.

Why the Market Needs GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS)

The recent explosion in generative models like ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, and custom enterprise-grade LLMs has triggered a gold rush in GPU demand. But most research laboratories and research centers and even mid-sized organizations cannot afford and maintain clusters of H100s or the next Blackwell chips. Not only do they come at a high cost, with prices well in excess of $30,000 per chip, but they require high-end cooling systems, high-throughput networks, and skilled engineers to service and maintain them. That’s where GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) fits in.

GPUaaS allows companies to “lease” access to high-end GPUs on-demand without having to pay up-front CapEx or costly infrastructure buildouts. It is particularly crucial in a world where it costs millions to train high-end models. Instead of investing in hardware, developers can focus on model development, data quality, and deployment. The GPUaaS architecture provides the compute foundation for the AI economy, scalable, agile, and capital-efficient to the client.

Along with that, hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT) Azure or Amazon (AMZN) AWS bundle GPU access with long-term contracts, usage lock-ins, and cost premiums. It presents a chance to GPUaaS providers like Nebius to offer faster onboarding, transparent costs, and better performance per dollar. With demand for AI in all sectors from pharmaceutical research to autonomous systems on the rise, the GPUaaS layer becomes a requirement and not an extravagance.

This is why the GPUaaS market will rise to ~$100 billion in the next several years, with much of the growth going to those who can offer predictable throughput, sovereign data ownership, and cost-effectiveness. Whoever possesses the most performant, adaptable, and cost-effective GPU layer in that game will have the future in its hand in terms of the AI infrastructure and Nebius is positioning itself as one of the only serious contenders outside the hyperscaler oligopoly.

The New Cloud Goldmine

These examples illustrate why GPUaaS with its high up-front cost will be worth it in the end. Look at AWS (Amazon Web Services) in the early 2000s, a capital-intensive gamble. Amazon invested in massive data centers and abstracted computing into an on-demand service provided to developers. Today the internet runs on AWS and provides more than 60% of Amazon’s operating income. That model, owning and monetizing compute capacity, turned CapEx into high-margin recurring revenue. Nebius’s GPUaaS play follows the same script with a twist focused on AI compute and not generic cloud.

Another example in point is Nvidia itself. Once a gaming GPU firm only, Nvidia made significant investments in CUDA programming software and tools and in partnerships within the ecosystem. The initial investment created a self-sustaining cycle where there were increasingly more and more models built on Nvidia hardware, locking in demand. The data center business is now the largest and fastest-growing business at Nvidia based on the GPUaaS-like dependence that workloads in AI have on its platform.

These examples show that investing in the “picks and shovels” of the next tech cycle, whether cloud computing in the 2000s, AI in the 2020s, or the next thing, can be very profitable if timing and execution go in your favor. Nebius aims to recreate that model in sovereign and developing markets where demand precedes the hyperscalers and trust in U.S.-controlled infrastructure is dwindling.

Infrastructure Innovation: Embracing Liquid Cooling to Enhance Efficiency

Nebius stands apart through ownership and optimization of physical space, a departure from the typical industry model based on third-party colocation data centers. Adopting the models used by Baidu and Tencent, Nebius has developed proprietary high-density data centers, several of which have been constructed underground to be space- and thermal-efficient. A feature of the facilities is the application of high-end liquid cooling systems.

Nebius data centers in Russia and Serbia are among the region’s most GPU-optimized data centers due to the emphasis on liquid cooling technology. With 45U custom racks with direct-to-chip cooling plates and extremely low air gaps, the data centers have near-peak GPU utilization with no thermal throttling risks involved. The architecture not only increases performance but also results in significant cost reductions in operations. The liquid cooling cost will be up to 30% lower in operational costs within a five-year time frame even with intensive AI training workloads.

Furthermore, Nebius real estate and electrical infrastructure ownership protects the company from fluctuations in the cost of power, an issue in European and U.S. data center markets that will grow in saliency in the future. The vertical integration strategy underscores Nebius’s commitment to cost containment and operational efficiency and places the company in a favorable position compared to competitors with higher operating costs due to facility leasing.

AI Service Expansion: From Infrastructure to Sovereign AI Solutions

In addition to its hardware developments, Nebius is expanding its footprint in the space of AI services with the development of an end-to-end stack of AI that matches and in multiple places exceeds that offered by Western hyperscalers. The company has tuned models such as LLaMA 2 and Falcon to support Cyrillic and Balkan languages to fill a significant void in regionally targeted AI solutions. Nebius is developing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) APIs designed to enhance the accuracy and contextuality of AI-generated content to meet the needs of users in the region.

A central element of Nebius’s strategy is its focus on providing secure AI-as-a-service solutions that allow customers to run inference workloads across sovereign or classified data without exposure to foreign jurisdictions. Its “red-teamed sovereignty stack” features containerized LLMs, air-gapped orchestration, and post-quantum encryption layers, analogous to early deployments by companies like Palantir (PLTR) but with an emphasis on in-country control. The strategy has attracted customers like Russia and Eastern European state-affiliated universities and defense contractors and established a regional market for AI development.

Takeaway

As the AI arms race evolves into one focused on survivability and local control, Nebius offers a compelling model for sustainable growth. Its innovative approaches to GPU sourcing, infrastructural design, and delivery of AI services not only differentiate it from the pack but lay a strong foundation upon which to expand in the years to come. To investors and market observers alike, Nebius is a lesson in the manner in which agility, forward vision, and technological innovation can coalesce to produce a strong competitor in the market for AI infrastructure.

data.title: Prada Buys Versace From Capri: Who Gains The Most?
data.description:
In my last article on Prada S.p.A. (OTCPK:PRDSF) I emphasized how solid this company was and how much it was undervalued compared to its fair value. Towards the end, I briefly mentioned the possibility of buying the Versace and Jimmy Choo brands, and I was quite excited about it. Those rumors have now become official, and Prada has entered into an agreement with Capri Holdings to buy Versace for $1.37 billion; Jimmy Choo will not be part of the agreement. But who stands to gain more between the two companies?

Prada has excellent reasons for buying Versace, but at the same time it seems that Capri Holdings Limited (NYSE:CPRI) won't be weakened by the agreement. In this article I'll show you the point of view of the two companies, and who I think will gain the most.

Capri Holdings: pros and cons of the sale

From a purely economic point of view, the sale of Versace for $1.37 billion cannot be considered a success in any way given that the Italian brand had been purchased for $2.10 billion in 2018. A loss of $730 million in 7 years that becomes even more significant if we consider the opportunity cost.

Since it was purchased, the Versace brand has gradually deteriorated, leading to an operating loss and double-digit sales decline.

It is clear that Capri Holdings did not manage to make the most of it, and a series of mistakes were made in its management. The objective was to reposition the brand for a new growth cycle after disappointing years, but this was not achieved.

We also made missteps in some of our repositioning efforts. While elevating the assortment, we believed we removed too many unique Versace statement items. Additionally, we significantly reduced our offerings of products at entry-level luxury price points. This impacted our retail sales, but had a more significant impact on our wholesale business.

John D. Idol - Chairman and CEO.

The decision to reduce entry level price points was not appreciated by consumers, and the crisis in the entire sector has fueled negative sentiment towards Versace. Demand for luxury is driven mainly by China, and since the latter is still overcoming the real estate crisis, there is a lack of lifeblood for this industry. In such a complex period, only brands with a strong competitive advantage can continue to grow, and Versace has not proven to be among them. Furthermore, the presence of a constant operating loss suggests that the brand has never really integrated itself within the synergies of the company.

In the last quarter, Versace sales represented only 15% of the group's total sales, while Michael Kors sales were 72%; the latter is the only brand that has seen an operating profit in the last quarter. Management is much more focused on the latter, but we are talking about a totally different market compared to Versace. The former deals mainly with handbags in the medium-to-high price range, while the latter is a high-end, long-standing brand that makes a wide range of products.

A few months ago, management's desire to join Tapestry was a clear signal of its new focus: to dominate the medium-to-high-end handbag market. The goal was to gain control over the two most popular brands in this price range, Coach and Michael Kors, but the authorities did not approve the deal. Versace is a heavy legacy to support, and Capri Holdings has not been able to profit from it as it did with Michael Kors.

Selling a brand for $730 million less than what you paid for it leaves a bitter taste, but at this rate Versace would have depreciated even more. It's understandable that Capri let it go, and with the incoming cash it will be able to both invest more in its main brand (Michael Kors) and improve its financial position. Its net debt is $2.76 billion and its operating income has proven to be rather unstable in recent years.

All in all, it's not an ideal situation from a financial point of view, but the $1.37 billion in proceeds will make it more stable and able to invest in the long term. In addition, Versace's operating loss, which weighs on the net profit, will be removed.

So, on the one hand Capri Holdings is losing a historic brand that didn't perform as hoped, but on the other hand it will improve its financial structure and be able to focus more on Michael Kors.

Prada: pros and cons of the purchase

The biggest pro of this acquisition is quite evident: Prada bought Versace at a bargain price compared to its potential. The two parties had reached an agreement for $1.6 billion, but following the widespread fear caused by Trump's tariffs, Versace's value dropped by another $200 million. As mentioned, Versace had been purchased for $2.1 billion in 2018.

In my opinion, Prada's decision to purchase this brand at this particular moment in time is not accidental, but part of a very specific strategy. The management has been very clever in exploiting the current market panic to obtain a considerable discount on the purchase price. Clearly, Versace will have to stop reporting operating losses to be considered a successful acquisition, but it has all the credentials to succeed.

In the high fashion industry, not many brands have a competitive advantage because it takes decades to cement consumer perception. Versace is a brand that was created in the late 70s, has a history of success behind it, and is still very popular despite everything. At $1.37 billion, I don't think Prada could have bought a better brand at the moment, and the potential return on investment is very high in my opinion.

With Versace, Prada is starting to become a true Italian giant of high fashion, as it also owns the Church's and Miu Miu brands. It can now play on equal terms with Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF) and give the LVMH group some tough competition.

Among other things, despite the negative period for the entire industry, its brands have all shown sales growth, which demonstrates how competent the management is. Miu Miu has almost achieved triple-digit growth and is a brand that is becoming increasingly premium.

Unlike Capri, which seems better suited to developing the potential of mid-range brands, Prada is more effective at exploiting high-end brands. For this reason, I am quite optimistic about this acquisition. Furthermore, the addition of Versace will improve revenue diversification and reduce the excessive concentration on the Prada brand.

In the initial stages Versace will drag down operating income, but this should only be a temporary problem. Once integrated into the company, the benefits should be considerable. Clearly, there is no guarantee that Versace will return to its former glory and this is the main risk.

Prada's management is very competent, but this is not a risk-free investment; a huge amount of work is needed to ensure that Versace starts being profitable again with double-digit margins. Consider that Prague and Versace are totally different from a stylistic point of view: the former aims for minimalism, the latter is eccentric and has a thousand nuances.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe this deal is beneficial for both companies:

  • Capri Holdings gets rid of a brand that it failed to make the most of, and with the proceeds it will reduce its debt and invest in its core brand.
  • Prada buys an internationally renowned brand at a discount and will integrate it into an already strong portfolio.

If I had to choose who benefited the most, I would say Prada. The timing of the acquisition was perfect, because the entire sector is in crisis and Trump's tariffs have fueled panic. Versace's value has dropped significantly, and for only $1.37 billion it has added a brand with great potential to its portfolio. A potential turnaround could make Prada an even bigger giant in the industry than it already is, and definitively detach Kering's competition.

The acquisition of Versace marks another step in the evolutionary journey of our Group, adding a new dimension, different and complementary. The Group’s infrastructure is strong, we have verticalized our brands’ organizations and reinforced our routines and processes. We feel ready to open this new chapter. Versace has huge potential. The journey will be long and will require disciplined execution and patience. The evolution of a brand always needs time and constant focus. I would like to thank Capri Holdings for having preserved and enhanced the heritage of this wonderful brand. Notwithstanding the sector uncertainties, we look at the future with confidence, focused on a long-term strategic vision.

Andrea Guerra, Group Chief Executive Officer.

There is a risk of failing, but the safety margin is high given the low price paid. The acquisition is expected to close in the second half of 2025, but regulatory approval is still pending. Should it be approved, Prada is ready to carve out an even bigger space for itself in high fashion and Capri Holdings is poised to make a strong commitment to Michael Kors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Two High Yielding REITs Going From Bargains To Screaming Buys
data.description:
One of the benefits of the recent market selloff is the emergence of new opportunities to deploy capital at higher yields.

The logic is simple, the lower the share price, the higher yield provided that the underlying dividend remains unchanged. The last part of that sentence is critical as, theoretically, we should appreciate the market's rationality and that it prices the assets based on the future cash flows. So, having higher yielding opportunities now should mean that the risk level in the system has gone up (alternatively, the future cash flows streams have decreased due to, say, increased costs, lowered demand etc.).

In my view, all of this is fair and it is only logical that the tariffs have pushed down the valuations for almost all equities out there.

Yet, there are areas and specific securities that before the tariff risk materialized were trading at already relatively depressed valuations.

One such an asset class is equity REITs. Before tariff-induced roller coasters started, REITs, in general, were dealing with inflation and interest rate uncertainty that held the valuations back from a decent recovery. Plus, the market was so consumed with the growth-biased and "risk-on" assets that many undervalued REITs remained simply under the radar.

What is interesting is that even with growth and the S&P 500 (SPY) trading at very rich multiples, the real estate market (VNQ), which was far from being overvalued, has still suffered heavier losses.

One major reason for this is the surge in the 10-year yield volatility that makes it quite difficult for REITs to a) embark on accretive M&As (as the stabilized WACC is unknown) and b) model new debt service cash flows that would come from bond refinancings.

Plus, as the REIT universe is rather heterogeneous, there are specific sectors that have pushed the VNQ down way more than other.

Two of them include hotels and health care REITs. And in these sectors, by being extremely selective, we can scope some very enticing buying opportunities.

Here are two REITs (1 health care and 1 hotel/lodging), which, in my view, have gone from bargains to screaming buys.

#1 Global Medical - 12.0%

Global Medical (NYSE:GMRE) is a small-cap medical office building REIT that has been punished very hard by the market. Currently, it trades at a P/FFO of 8.1x, which is the lowest level it has had except for the COVID-19 period. The multiple has been already depressed going into the Trump's Presidency.

In the chart below, we can see how huge the difference between GMRE and VNQ there has been since the Fed started to hike the interest rates.

On a YTD basis, GMRE has also underperformed VNQ by ~300 basis points.

So, what are the main reasons for this?

First, GMRE as a small-cap REIT is per definition deemed riskier than large cap ones that can access debt financing at better and more flexible terms.

Second, most of the properties are spread across the secondary and tertiary markets, which, theoretically, increase the risk.

Third, many investors draw pareales to Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW), which has been a complete disaster due to two major tenants going belly up.

Fourth, the underlying fundamentals are not best-in-class.

Fifth, and this is really summing the four pieces above together and contextualizing them with the prevailing market dynamics: i.e., assuming risk in the last thing that many investors want now.

While I agree that GMRE should deserve a below-average P/FFO multiple, P/FFO of 8.1x seems just wrong and overly aggressive.

Here is why.

Even though GMRE is a small-cap REIT it is well-diversified, owning 190 building that are distributed across 37 States. The lion's share of GMRE's medical office buildings are leased out on either triple-net or absolute lease basis that help the REIT avoid headwinds on the OpEx front.

Since the IPO, GMRE's portfolio has always remained strongly occupied with very limited provisions for non-performing tenants. As of now, the occupancy is at 96.4%. Speaking of the portfolio, we have to factor in the portfolio rent coverage, which is a clear indicative of a high-quality tenant base. Namely, the portfolio rent coverage is 4.5x and it has trended upwards over the past couple of quarters.

In the text above, I mentioned that GMRE has not the greatest fundamentals out there. It is because of two reasons:

  1. AFFO payout ratio of 95%
  2. Fixed rate 2.8% debt maturity in mid 2026.

So, the AFFO payout ratio looks quite high and given the 2026 debt maturity, if things do not improve, we could make a case for a dividend cut.

However, GMRE had a couple of similar debt refinancing events in 2023 and 2024 period, and the AFFO per share remained perfectly stable. The thing is that GMRE enjoys annual rent escalators of ~2%, which in combination with accretive asset recycling activity stimulate the AFFO growth nicely. For example, in Q4, 2024 GMRE sold ~$40 million of assets at a low 7% cap rate (in early 2024 it had sales at 5% cap rate). Then GMRE took these proceeds and combined with an additional debt to acquire a ~$69 million asset at a cap rate of 9%. This is how GMRE can create and/or preserve value, while issuing fresh equity is not an option at these valuations.

In a nutshell, GMRE is higher quality REIT than what we can, currently, imply from the market (i.e., the multiple). Sure, the dividend is not the safest out there, but at a P/FFO of 8.1x, 12% yield and the aforementioned fundamentals, GMRE is a bargain.

#2 Apple Hospitality - 8.9%

Apple Hospitality (NYSE:APLE) is a pure-play hotel/lodging REIT, which trades at a similar dirt cheap multiple as GMRE. Currently, the P/FFO stands at 7.3x and the dividend yield is 8.9%.

While APLE's valuations have never been high, what we have seen happening since mid February is a complete meltdown in the market cap. APLE has underperformed VNQ by ~19% on a YTD basis. It is down ~25% this year.

Here, the logic behind the plunge is simple.

The key driver for APLE's depressed share price is the assumption of an recessionary environment and the knock-on effects to the economy. The idea is that if we are in the recession, hotels will be one of the first items, where both corporates and individuals will try to turn light off in order to save some cash flows.

I do not think that we have something to argue against such a scenario (even though it is still unclear whether we will really experience a painful and/or prolonged recession).

It is completely normal to see cyclical stocks like hotel REITs get hammered under such uncertainty levels that we have now.

Having said that, just as I think that the market has gone too far by penalizing GMRE at this extent, in APLE's case the situation is, in my opinion, the same.

Here is why.

First, APLE runs a rooms-focused business model, which is, generally, more defensive than the competing strategies. Plus, it tends to be less CaPex and OpEx-intensive, which, in turn, offers a greater margin of safety to handle the pressures on the cash flows.

Second, an additional layer of defense comes from the fact that APLE's portfolio is quite fresh and exposed to favorable supply/demand dynamics. For instance, the average effective age of APLE buildings is ~5 years. The risk of new supply weakening the positioning of APLE's portfolio is the lowest it has been since 2019.

Third, APLE has, arguably, one of the best financials in the hotel REIT sector. The net debt to total capitalization is only 28%. Net debt to EBITDA is around 3.0x, which is much lower than what we can see for other (even blue-chip REITs) - i.e., usually, between 5.0x to 6.0x.

Finally, APLE carries a robust cash flow (MFFO) payout ratio of 61%. In my opinio, this should be more than enough to absorb any forthcoming declines that might arise from a weaker demand. In fact, I would not be surprised if APLE embarks on accretive share buybacks, using the undistributed MFFO and some prudent amount of leverage to acquire its stock at P/FFO of 7.2x.

The bottom line

The heightened volatility has created interesting opportunities across the board. The REIT universe is particularly attractive as it has both lagged behind the S&P 500 before the tariffs and continued to underperform after they got announced.

In this article, I have discussed two 9%+ yielding REITs, which are backed by fundamentals that are much stronger than what the valuations multiples indicate.

data.title: DigitalOcean: Don't Miss The Pivot
data.description:
The DigitalOcean (NYSE:DOCN) investment thesis hinges two critical factors. Firstly, a renewed leadership team that’s driving a product led growth strategy combined with a push for higher net-retention-rate through innovative solutions in the SMB market. The second factor is a meaningful margin acceleration, which combined with strong growth will likely lead to 27% IRR in 2027 or 60% upside from current trading levels.

Overview of the business

Established in 2012, Digital Ocean’s core mission is to simplify cloud computing and artificial intelligence for developers and tech companies. Its cornerstone offering called Droplet is a virtual machine that offers flexibility to build, test, secure, grow and scale applications on the cloud. Droplets are essentially Linux-based virtual machines that run on top of virtualized hardware. Each Droplet you create is a new server, either standalone or as part of a larger, cloud-based infrastructure.

The company focuses on simplicity and ease of use. Different configurations are optimized for different needs according to the scope in question - such as general purpose computing, CPU intensive tasks, memory optimization or storage optimization. Over time, the management team has strategically expanded its portfolio through some organic product innovation and M&A activity, such as Paperspace and Cloudways , to offer a comprehensive suite of cloud services spanning Infrastructure as a Service , Platform as a Service, Software as a Service, and machine learning & AI solutions.

Inherent to its product led growth strategy is its self service platform whereby customers , primarily developers in the SMB field, can manage cloud resources with easy of access and transparent through a predictable monthly consumption model. Unlike the major hyper-scalers such as Amazon’s AWS, Microsoft’s Azure, and Alphabet’s GCP, which provide a massive array and often overwhelming selection of products and features for enterprise level usage, DOCN focuses on serving core functionality, infrastructure and platform needed for SMBs. The so called “hyper-scaler lite” doesn't mean a lack of capability but a laser focus on simplicity and accessibility of usage. This simplicity offer superior total cost of ownership economics for customers.

DOCN’s geographical sales footprint is split into 38% US, 28% Europe, 24% Asia and 10% Rest of World. My assumptions in the proceeding valuation framework are underpinned by a series of arguments that I lay out below.

Growth

To understand the future, we need to have a decent understanding of what happened in the past. Coming out of the COVID digital boom, DigitalOcean enjoyed a big wave of organic growth from its frictionless product led growth strategy. The customer-centric development mindset fuelled solid growth of new customers and the installed base grew healthily to now reach 638K. Most customers land on the simplest tier and then it’s all about landing and expanding. However, after the headwinds in the tech world of 2022, organic growth decelerated from 20s% in the previous years to 7% and Net Dollar Retention Rates troughing at 96.3% in Q3 23. Besides the macro headwinds, management also acknowledged that they underinvested for a period of time in product innovation, particularly in features required by scaling or scaled customers which further contributed to the dip. The cherry on the cake was the lapsing comps of price increases that happened in 2022.

The next chapter of this growth began with the strategic pivot initiated by the new CEO Paddy Srinivasanstory appointed in Feb 2024. Since then, the focus is on "double-click and deliver on our core cloud computing platform" and to "jumpstart AI" (UBS conference). As part of that, the new CEO put heavier emphasis on product innovation, especially for scaler customers, spending over $25K annually, customers that were somewhat neglected in the past 2 years. This customer cohort collectively drives 36% of total revenue in Q4 24. High spend customers account for 88% of revenue and grew 6% YoY in Q424, while ARR grew 16% signalling good product uptake and organic growth.

Looking ahead in 2025, the company guides for full year revenue growth in the mid-teens with 13% being the midpoint. From the recent investor day, the it’s clear that internal momentum is strong despite potential macro headwinds. Net dollar retention ("NDR") is on the rise, hitting 99% in Q4 24 and even stronger at 100% for core cloud. The re-organization and initiatives are already paying off. The NDR target for 2025 is 100% and I foresee management beating that target as the scalers cohort of customers continues to grow fast. The early adoption of their GPU droplets and GenAI platform is dictating their differentiated, inference-focused strategy.

Much like many other product led growth companies, the plan over the medium term is to start landing bigger deals on day 1. As the company is developing enterprise-grade features such as advanced networking, it is making a compelling total cost of ownership argument as its at-least 30% cheaper than hyper-scalers according to their insights share in the investor April investor day. I expect organic growth to reaccelerate to 18-20% by 2027 driven by new customer acquisition and healthy expansion with the inflection in NDR outlined above. There are already strong signs in the large customer cohorts with NDR improving over 18%.

Over the next five to seven years, I expect organic growth in the mid teens coming from the vast potential hidden inside of the current and growing customer base. Digital ocean’s focus on reducing complexity, providing ease of use and reducing total cost of ownership fits the market’s needs. Admittedly, they dropped the ball on product innovation and execution over the past couple of years but structural and organisational changes have been made to address these. DigitalOcean's long-term strategy is underpinned by a key pillar, that the cloud market, despite its vast size, is daunting and leaving customers behind due to increasing complexity and cost. The company’s cost effective and easy to use solution avoids direct confrontations and price wars against hyper-scalers. Even though competitors like Vultr compete on price and some performance metrics, DigitalOcean’s user experience, reliability and established developer community act as strong defences.

Margin profile and forecast

Looking back in late 2021, the company had a solid customer base and even raised prices. That made for a robust gross margin of roughly 63%. Coming out of the macro headwinds in 2022, the company posted 57% gross margins in 2023. During that period, customers optimized their cloud spend and tried to do more with less. In 2024, the arrival of new leadership and their initiatives re-accelerated gross margins to 60%.

Looking ahead, I foresee gross margins heading back to mid-60s% with the new Atlanta data-centre creating cost efficiencies, and their higher margin platform as a service products, that are projected to be roughly 30% of core cloud revenue, growing significantly faster than basic infrastructure. On top of that, the AI platform has stronger margins that simple GPU infrastructure which will provide an additional uplift. Management is also allocating efforts to boost the efficiency of current infrastructure that will likely lead to roughly 200bps of gross margin expansion.

On the operating front, I foresee adj. EBITDA holding strong in the low/mid 40s % while the company is investing in going up-market to reaccelerate organic revenue growth which will eventually lead to operating leverage. As the capex outlays for AI infrastructure start to ease, I foresee the free cashflow margin going high teens or low 20s. To sum up the margin story, it’s one that evolved from a mis-prioritized management and imperfect efficiency to one of higher efficiency, targeted investments in high value pockets like PaaS and AI, and infrastructure optimization.

Model and risks

The assumptions for my modelling have been explained in the preceding body of text. In fact my expectations align with consensus figures for this year and 2026, but diverge slightly from consensus on the upside for both growth and profitability for the combined rationale outlined above. Regarding the selected exit multiple, I prefer to be conservative and model for 13x which is just a touch below the two-year average of 13.8x and well below the 17x reached at peak. With that, my model leads me to a 27% IRR and roughly 60% return from current levels.

The first and most important risk to discuss is competition. Although DigitalOcean focuses on SMBs, hyper-scalers are always a force to be reckoned and should not be neglected just because of the TCO argument. In the unlikely case that they choose to simplify down their offerings and cut their prices, they could effectively steal DOCN's right to win. Management is not trying to out-muscle the hyper-scalers but rather focus on their own value proposition while innovating on the edges to mitigate this risk.

Execution risk is always inherent with strategic pivots and management changes such as this one. A product-led growth strategy in combination with an up-market push can be tricky to navigate. However, I believe the new leadership team is sufficiently capable to execute especially considering their background in leading companies such as Microsoft, Amazon and Oracle. We are also at a point in the cycle where companies are seeking to do more with less while keeping TCO in check. This bodes well with DigitalOcean's offering.

Finally, sentiment shifts such as the recent one can introduce significant volatility in the stock but also in deal flow as executive's confidence flows with general sentiment in the market. Despite that, DigitalOcean has weathered the storm of 2022 and is structurally well positioned to capitalize on the rise of AI and the rising wave of SMBs in this new era.

data.title: 3D Systems: Restructuring And The Challenging Path To Turnaround
data.description:
3D Systems (NYSE: NYSE:DDD) is one of the leading global 3D printer manufacturers. As I anticipated in the Nano Dimension (NNDM) article, this market is facing challenging conditions due to the weakness of the manufacturing sector and a macroeconomic environment characterized by high interest rates, which discourage company investments. These dynamics have adversely impacted the financial performance of 3D Systems, which between FY21 and FY24 recorded a -10.5% revenue CAGR, declining operating margins and a significant asset write-down.

These issues led management to undertake a corporate restructuring, aiming for a $50 million reduction in operating costs and an increased focus on the core business. For this purpose, in April 2025 it completed the sale of the Geomagic software to Hexagon for $123 million, a useful transaction to strengthen the financial position. Nevertheless, I believe that reaching breakeven requires a clear rebound in the industry and a consequent increase in revenue and sales volume, which is the only way to achieve positive long-term operating margins.

Comparison with the two most important competitors, Stratasys (SSYS) and Nano Dimension, highlights the weakness of 3D Systems compared to its peers in terms of revenue, margins and debt. The significant slump in the stock price over the past few years and the potential for industry recovery due to reshoring led me to assign 3D Systems a "hold" rating, despite an outlook that, in my opinion, is not particularly optimistic.

Business Overview

3D Systems sells products and offers services related to additive manufacturing with a globally diversified portfolio. In FY24, it generated 57.59% of sales in the Americas, 34.02% in EMEA, and 8.39% in APAC. Between FY19 and FY24, the company experienced a general sales decline across all three regions in which it operates. The main products sold by the company are 3D printers, materials, software, scanners and haptic devices. Service offerings include hardware and software maintenance, training, advanced manufacturing and healthcare solutions.

In FY24, products accounted for 63.43% of revenue, down 15.7% compared to FY23, driven by a 9.6% decline in volume and a 5.2% decline in pricing and sales mix. I expect a decline in FY25 as well, as the sale of Geomagic is projected to negatively contribute about $30 million to product revenue. The decline in sales volumes and low market demand for 3D printers resulted in a lower gross profit margin, down to 37.01% from 38.17% in FY23.

Services, accounting for 36.57% of the company's revenue in FY24, exhibit the same dynamics from FY19 to FY24. However, services have more stable revenue, with a positive trend between FY22 and FY24, reporting a 1.01% increase in FY24 over FY23. Nevertheless, the gross profit margin continues to be in a negative trend, decreasing from 44.53% in FY23 to 37.81% in FY24. Comparison with the FY19 gross profit margin of 65.04% reveals the high loss in operating efficiency, which is greater compared to products segment.

The company's activities are divided into two segments. Healthcare Solutions, which includes end-markets such as dental, regenerative medicine, medical devices, and personalized health services, and Industrial Solutions, which mainly serves aerospace, defense, and transportation.

Healthcare Solutions

The Healthcare Solutions segment accounted for 43.11% of 3D Systems' revenue in FY24, dropping 11.01% YoY. The decline in sales caused a slight reduction in the gross profit margin, to 38.74% in FY24 from 39.94% in FY23, though it shows a stable trend between FY22 and FY24. Management focused heavily on the business unit growth strategy in the Q4 investor presentation, identifying it as the company's main investment area, with particular emphasis on the dental market, forecasting a US addressable market valued at $1 billion by 2029. In FY24, it also unveiled NextDent 300, a 3D printer that fabricates one-piece removable dental prostheses with an integrated digital workflow in less than 24 hours. The product is scheduled for commercial release in FY25 and is expected to contribute positively to segment sales.

Another area of interest is Regenerative Medicine, dedicated to human organ transplantation. However, these investments, made alongside some strategic partners, focus only on R&D activities and don’t have, at the moment, a material impact on the company's financial results, except in terms of costs. Furthermore, in Q1 FY24, due to a loss of funding from partners, the research programs regarding liver and kidney were discontinued. Considering these developments, I believe that the disclosure of these activities acts more for reputation purposes than for actual potential economic value.

Industrial Solutions

The Industrial Solutions segment accounted for 56.89% of 3D Systems' FY24 revenue, down 8.9% YoY, although it performed well in Q4 FY24, where it reported 11% QoQ growth, led by aerospace and defense demand. Between FY21 and FY24, the business unit reported a milder revenue decline than Healthcare Solutions, despite a larger drop in gross profit margin. Therefore, I believe the company chose to maintain a higher market share at the expense of margins by lowering the price of products and services sold. It reported a gross profit margin of 36.22% in FY24, significantly below 40.48% in FY23.

The rising use of additive manufacturing within corporate production processes could drive revenue growth and margin improvement in the medium to long term, but weakness in the manufacturing sector makes this a difficult target in the short term. For these reasons, CEO Graves expects the segment to perform flat or slightly positive in FY25.

Will Restructuring Lead To Breakeven?

Management announced a $50 million cost-cutting program by FY26 based on several initiatives: the search for cheaper material solutions, factory utilization improvements, better inventory controls and logistics efficiencies, and finally, a reduction in sales costs and back-office streamlining, translating into a staff reduction. Even if these goals were to succeed, a $50 million reduction would not be sufficient to achieve breakeven, considering that, even excluding the asset impairment loss of $144 million, the company would have reported negative EBIT of $133 million in FY24.

In my view, the only realistic chance of delivering positive EBIT depends on a return to growth in products revenue sufficient to take advantage of economies of scale. This is possible, as the global 3D printing industry, valued at $19.3 billion in 2024, is expected to exceed $100 billion by 2032, with a 23.4% CAGR. Factors such as reshoring and increased spending in defense and aerospace can be two key drivers for additive manufacturing market growth, especially in the US. However, there are certain risks to achieving revenue growth. The 3D printing industry is a competitive sector, with pure players such as Stratasys and Nano Dimension and multinational such as HP (HPQ) and General Electric (through Colibrium Additive), which have far more resources and investment capacity than 3D Systems.

The $123 million obtained from the sale of Geomagic could be used to boost investment and spending on R&D, strengthening the competitive position of the company. Nevertheless, 3D Systems' history shows that it has always favored inorganic growth, making multiple acquisitions. Between FY21 and FY23, it spent $272 million on acquisitions, reporting a significant decline in revenue and margins in the same period. These transactions had as their only material impact a massive build-up of debt, with net debt rising from $-287 million as of December 2021 to $102 million as of December 2024, and extremely high goodwill impairments, a clear indicator of overpriced acquisitions compared to their fair value.

For these reasons, unless there is a radical and rapid change in management strategy, I believe a sharp increase in revenue in the short term is unlikely, and that the $50 million cost reduction will hardly be sufficient to ensure positive operating margins and profitability, implying further cash outflows and increased debt.

Comparison With Peers

The comparison with Stratasys and Nano Dimension, the two main rivals in the US market, confirms the arguments discussed above. As of January 2022, the stock price of 3D Systems declined by 86.42%, exceeding Stratasys and Nano Dimension, which dropped by 58.82% and 56.67%, respectively.

In FY19 and FY20, 3D Systems' revenues were aligned with that of Stratasys. However, from FY21 to FY24, the latter focused more on organic R&D and CAPEX, using only $92 million for acquisitions; hence, it was able to record stable revenue even in a weak market environment. In contrast, 3D Systems' revenue showed a steady decline despite $272 million spent on acquisitions. A similar trend was evident when considering gross profit margins. Both Stratasys and Nano Dimension have seen consistent margin expansion between FY22 and FY24, reaching 47.3% and 48.5% respectively, above 3D Systems' 37.1%.

The balance-sheet also shows a large divergence among the three companies. While Stratasys and Nano Dimension hold negative net debt, 3D Systems has net debt approximately equivalent to the company's current market cap, indicating less capacity to make new investments, which are crucial for an early-stage market such as that of additive manufacturing.

The price to sales ratio, the only measure that can be used for comparison, shows that 3D Systems' P/S is significantly lower than that of both Stratasys and Nano Dimension. Although this result may portend higher upside potential for 3D Systems, it also demonstrates the company's low valuation and the bearishness of investors toward it.

Main Risks

3D Systems had equity of $176 million as of December 2024, steadily declining due to large losses reported in the income statement. The same applies to available liquidity, which has been affected by negative OCF between FY22 and FY24.

3D Systems had $212 million in convertible debt as of December 2024, owed to The Bank of New York Mellon, maturing on November 15, 2026. Repaying the debt would deplete the company's available liquidity, even considering the sale of Geomagic. Conversely, any conversion would have highly dilutive effects for 3D Systems' shareholders.

The 3D printing market is experiencing a continuous increase in competition and requires substantial R&D investment to maintain its competitive position.

A trade war between Europe and the US, although it doesn't seem the most likely scenario at the moment, could have a negative impact on 3D Systems' bottom line, which generated 34% of revenue in EMEA in FY24.

Take-Away

3D Systems is suffering from a steady decline in revenue and margins, due to both the weakness in the manufacturing sector and management's strategy of growth through acquisitions that are far too expensive relative to their actual fair value. This strategy has led to a significant drop in available cash, an increase in debt, and a sharp write-down of assets. Although the corporate restructuring plan might foster a slight improvement in the short term, in the long run, the only way out lies in a substantial increase in sales. A comparison with Stratasys and Nano Dimension further highlights how much the company has underperformed its competitors. Considering the steep decline in stock price in recent years and the potential for improvement in the US manufacturing sector, I assign 3D Systems a "hold" rating.

data.title: United Airlines Can Weather This Storm - Earnings Preview (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The U.S. airline industry came out of the post covid gate running as soon as demand began to return in 2022. United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) made the decision during covid to not retire any aircraft and to manage its staffing so that it could take advantage of demand when it returned. Although each of American (AAL), Delta (DAL) and Southwest (LUV) have larger domestic route systems than UAL and domestic travel returned before international travel post-covid, United wanted to make sure it was well-positioned to grow its international network. Pre-covid, Delta was the largest carrier across the Atlantic based on capacity but United overtook Delta post-covid. United has been the largest airline across the Pacific, regardless of nationality, for about 10 years. Since Asian travel was slower to restart than within the U.S. or in Latin America or Europe, United was well-positioned to grow its transpacific presence particularly against Asian carriers. It set out bold plans to accelerate its growth via massive aircraft orders and airport terminal expansions. United’s margins began to rise closer to Delta which has been the U.S. market leader in revenue, profits, and market capitalization. I identified a number of risks in this Seeking Alpha article just two months ago expressing my fears that UAL’s stock ascent could not continue. The entire stock market has faced enormous turbulence since the beginning of the year with UAL losing one-third of its value so it is worth re-evaluating my rating on UAL.

Industry Context

Delta released its first quarter earnings on Wednesday April 9. DAL is typically the first major airline in the world and one of the first large U.S. consumer companies to report so its report always attracts attention. Its earnings were generally in line with its previously revised guidance but it pulled its full year guidance based on uncertainty esp. around trade and tariffs. It noted weakness in the domestic coach market and says that it is fairly comfortable with international and domestic demand through the second quarter which heavily includes outbound travel by Americans to Europe but is less certain about the demand profile after the middle of August when many American schools reopen for the new school year. It plans to reduce capacity in the second quarter with an eye to even further reductions in the second half of the year.

In light of Delta’s commentary, all eyes will be on United’s guidance for the second quarter and its potential commentary and guidance for the second quarter and beyond. Based on my expectations and considering expected airline industry performance, I will evaluate United’s previously outlined plans and strategies which are generally more growth and expansion oriented than Delta’s.

In considering the industry backdrop, it is important to note that UAL’s financial position relative to the industry has improved during the post-covid period. Premium and international travel has provided considerable strength to Delta and United who lead the industry in those segments. Domestic leisure travel has been under pressure since the earliest days of the covid recovery. U.S. airlines were heavily subsidized during covid through government grants that required airlines to retain employees in return for covering their salary and benefits costs. There has been overcapacity in the domestic sector since covid. Inflation has weakened buying power esp. for lower income consumers. Airline labor costs have increased faster than capacity, resulting in deteriorating labor cost efficiency metrics. It is not a surprise that many of the low cost and ultra-low-cost U.S. airlines are operating at low margins, if they are profitable at all. While American is a global carrier like Delta and United, AAL’s margins have been below low-cost carrier Southwest.

Preying on the weak

United has been very vocal about attempting to win over passengers that low and ultra-low-cost carriers have used to fill their planes. United was the first network carrier to eliminate change fees during covid, a strategy that they positioned as making it easier for passengers to make travel plans during a period of health uncertainty; in reality, the move hasn’t been rescinded and is undoubtedly part of UAL’s success in winning over customers in major cities like Chicago, Denver and Houston where LUV and UAL both have large operations. UAL execs also said they plan to fill extra seats on their larger aircraft as part of their domestic growth strategy with ultra-low-cost carrier passengers, offering them better value.

Spirit Airlines was the first economic casualty with a chapter 11 filing last year and re-emergence this under private equity control. However, Spirit has historically been larger in markets where American and Delta are larger. Delta has previously noted that it will continue to add capacity as necessary to essentially ensure that ultra-low cost carriers cannot grow but it is not clear how the Atlanta-based megacarrier will strategize around deep discount capacity after saying it will reduce capacity. United’s greatest overlap with an ultra-low cost carrier is with Frontier (ULCC) which has recently noted weakness beginning in May, leading to its plans to reduce capacity in the second quarter. ULCC also has little insight into the back half of the year.

The sudden gloom in the airline industry and the unfinished restructuring from covid on top of the restructuring of Spirit raises the question if there will be further consolidation in the U.S. airline industry. United execs expressed on their most recent earnings call that they would be interested in talking with JetBlue (JBLU) if the NYC-based carrier was open to discussions. JBLU has major operations at New York Kennedy, an airport that a previous UAL leadership team left in order to consolidate UAL’s long-haul presence at Newark. UAL’s current leadership team recognizes that leaving JFK airport was a mistake since it is the preferred NYC airport for long-haul travel. JBLU also is a major carrier at Boston and in Florida; while UAL touts its global size, it is the sixth largest airline in the Sunshine State. It is far from clear if further airline industry consolidation will be allowed by the current administration beyond the Alaska-Hawaii merger which just occurred but United appears to be ready to start plugging some of the holes in its network should opportunities arise.

Russian Airspace Reopening?

As the largest U.S. international airline, United has been the most impacted U.S. airline because of closure of Russian airspace to western airlines as a result of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. President Trump’s desire to end the conflict raises the potential for Russian airspace to be reopened, saving as much as two hours of flying time between parts of Asia and the U.S. In addition, many European and Asian airlines fly well south of the routings they used before the Ukraine war adding time and expense to flights between those regions.

Before the Russia-Ukraine war, United operated up to a half dozen flights per day from the U.S. to India but can only operate just a single flight/day between Newark and Delhi, India operated by a Boeing (BA) 787; the circuitous path that U.S.-India flights have to take pushes the capabilities of United’s fleet. The dashed line in the flight tracking map indicates the most direct path between Delhi and Newark while the green line indicates the actual flight path on a recent day, adding almost 800 miles and pushing the time in the air over 16 hours on some days. Much of the traffic that U.S. airlines cannot carry to India is instead flying via their European partner hubs, via Middle East hubs, or on Air India which does not respect Russia airspace embargoes. Pending the availability of aircraft, United is certain to add more India flights if Russian airspace reopens to U.S. carriers.

Lower fuel prices

United, like all airlines, is a heavy user of petroleum products. In 2024, UAL burned more than 4 billion gallons of jet fuel, costing almost $12 billion or roughly 22% of UAL’s expenses. However, 2024’s fuel bill was down 7% year over year and down 19% in the 4th quarter of 2024 even though it is consuming about 5% more fuel year over year. Delta’s fuel bill for the first quarter of 2025 was down 7%.

Crude oil has continued its decline in 2025, falling 14% since the beginning of the year and 10% in just the last month. Amidst revenue uncertainty, lower fuel costs could help minimize profit declines.

The disadvantages of a large global network

While United’s competitive position and the potential exists to add even more international flights to its impressive international route system, there are significant risks that United must overcome in the present environment. First among the risks facing United is the very likely decrease in international travel particularly to the United States from many of the countries that have historically been strong sources of U.S. tourism. The U.S. and Europe have historically experienced fairly balanced air traffic flows. While data is still fluid, there are growing indications that visitors from some countries are reducing their travel to the U.S. Tariff issues and U.S. remarks denigrating the sovereignty of Canada and Greenland have soured many Canadians and Danes on travel to the U.S. while foreign news has covered stories of immigrant expulsions and increased immigration interrogations at U.S. airports. Some sources indicate that foreign visitor arrivals may already be down by double digit percentages including from Germany, home of Lufthansa German Airlines (OTCQX:DLAKY), UAL’s joint venture and location of United’s largest European operations. The relatively strong dollar continues to support strong tourism by Americans to Europe which occurs generally in the first half of the summer; European tourism to the U.S. increases in the latter half of the summer and in the winter and might be at more risk. As the currently largest transatlantic carrier but with a smaller domestic network than its peers, United could experience a disproportionate decline in revenue if fears of declines in European tourism are sustained.

Travel between Asia and the U.S. is more heavily weighted to Asian originating passengers but the U.S. appears to be more conciliatory in talking about making trade deals with most Asian countries other than China. Flights between the U.S. and China have remained at a fraction of their pre-covid levels but demand could still fall further if relations and trade deteriorate further.

International travel has never been proportionately as profitable as domestic flights for U.S. airlines; United could have ridden a recent three year high in international demand that could be once again waning.

Financial commitments remain high

As I discussed in my previous article, United has retired a fairly low number of aircraft even though its fleet is one of the oldest in the world among major carriers. It has received far fewer aircraft than it had hoped but it also has an aircraft order book that is larger than the rest of the U.S. airline industry combined. It is only because Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) and Boeing have experienced delivery delays due to their own manufacturing or due to other suppliers including engine makers that United has not spent as much as it had previously planned to spend on capex. If UAL receives all of the aircraft it has on order for the next couple of years, its cash needs far exceed its projected available cash, meaning that taking on additional debt or reducing its cash is likely. Aircraft manufacturing supply chains are expected to see improvement in the next year meaning that United could see an inflow of new aircraft – and an increase in spending – just as the industry weakens. United is also invested tens of millions of dollars on new and expanded airport terminals to support its planned growth, but again far larger than the rest of the industry combined. Even if demand remains strong, United’s terminal expansion will result in higher user costs than for other airlines that are using older facilities.

UAL also has an outstanding stock buyback plan; the majority of the $1.5 billion remains outstanding unless the company is taking advantage of the decreased stock price to buyback stock.

United’s labor costs are certain to increase

Also, as I previously noted, United’s labor costs are likely to grow. By the summer, nearly all of its unionized non-pilot workforce will be working under amendable contracts. Airline labor contracts do not expire so unions negotiate with management until a new contract is reached. American was the most recent airline to settle with major labor groups, adding hundreds of millions of new expenses and paying over $500 million in retroactive payments to its flight attendants. United’s flight attendant workforce is similarly sized to American’s and represents about half of the United employees that could see substantial pay raises and retroactive payments. In addition, airline employees that believe they are underpaid become unhappy employees and customer service deteriorates.

An earnings beat is likely

UAL is expected to release its earnings after market close on Tuesday. Although other airlines have revised their first quarter guidance, UAL said its guidance would be at the low end of its guidance. UAL has a fairly strong history of beating analyst estimates over the last two years.

Wall Street analysts are the most optimistic about UAL while the Seeking Alpha Quant rating remains as a HOLD.

UAL stock has fallen 33% in less than two months since I wrote my last article about the company. Predictable near-term financial performance by Delta, United’s track record for beating analyst estimates, and UAL’s likely ability to weather the likely industry downturn better than its peers, even at the cost of balance sheet stress, merits a return of UAL to a BUY.

data.title: JAAA: A Safe Haven With Bond-Like Yields
data.description:
I recently wrote an article about JAAA, a Collateralised Loan Obligation ("CLO") fund, which I recommended as a smart way to generate a 6% yield. My proposal was to hedge interest rate risk by pairing with Realty Income (O) which has inverse rate risk exposure.

My original article provides a detailed explanation of CLOs, how they operate, and profiled the key risks. I won’t repeat that in this article, but would recommend interested readers click the link, not just for the article, but for a great discussion thread where there is a lot to learn.

JAAA is my preferred CLO investment, because it has a good balance of yield and risk. The current yield is just over 6%, and the risk profile is, as the name implies, AAA quality. This is possible because JAAA invests only in the highest tranches of CLOs which individually are rated AAA by the rating agencies.

The investment case is that JAAA is a great place to store cash, as it offers a spread over cash rates which is higher than the risk of loss of capital justifies.

The one catch, is that, compared to cash, CLO funds like JAAA are exposed to liquidity squeezes, which drop the market price of the fund below the Net Asset Value ("NAV").

The mechanism is, that when there is market stress, investors want, or need to access cash, perhaps to cover margin or other obligations, or simply to ‘raise cash and reduce risk’. Liquidity disappears from the marketplace like water down the plug-hole.

This showed through clearly in recent price action on JAAA around “Liberation Day” and the associated tariff driven market turmoil.

JAAA price has dropped about 1.7% since April 1st. Note however that there was a divided payment date April 1st which accounts for about 0.4% of the drop.

The rest of the drop is mostly due to the shift in market price from a slight premium to NAV, to a material discount. Look at how the discount bounced right back to a 0.6% premium on April 8-9th, and then dropped back to a 0.6% discount as of Friday 11th.

The shift mirrors the general ‘risk on-risk off’ market sentiment as reflected in the SPY pricing.

And you can see clearly the big spike in trading volume in the beginning of April.

The last view I want to show is the correlation between price and volume for JAAA.

From where I am sitting, this doesn’t make sense folks. For the price to NAV of a cash equivalent to bounce up and down like a yo-yo in line with daily market sentiment seems inexplicable.

It’s obvious to me that there is no sophisticated technical driver for this, such as concerns around the underlying CLO NAV valuation. As long as the yield spread to risk free is maintained, an investor can have high confidence that the market price will revert to NAV.

In fact, my expectation is that following such a disruptive event, and with bond yields moving up, its likely that CLO spreads will head up as well.

Conclusion

  • JAAA is a strong place to store cash with minimal risk and an out-sized return.
  • There’s a price tag to accessing that cash when the market is risk off and liquidity is at a premium.
  • This shows up in a temporary NAV discount which seems highly correlated to the market appetite for risk assets.
  • Seeing this work in practice has assuaged my concerns around the price drop in JAAA.
  • JAAA remains a buy as a low risk, high yield bond proxy.
  • A good time to buy is when there is a NAV discount, however the opportunity is tens of basis points rather than tens of percent.
